seo.title: GrowKind World — Education that grows with the child
seo.description: An educational and editorial project dedicated to neurodivergent childhood. A book, courses and resources for families and professionals who value understanding before intervention.

hero.subtitle: For parents and professionals who value understanding before intervention.
hero.slideHome: GrowKind World — A world built from the neurodivergent experience
hero.slideBook: Where the World Is Born Between Us — the book by João Pereira
hero.slideRdf: Relational Development Framework (RDF) — a new way of looking at development

origin.body1:
For a long time, the question was always the same:
how do we teach the child to adapt to the world?
origin.body2:
GrowKind is born from a different question:
how do we organise the world so that it makes sense
for different ways of existing?
origin.body3:
It is not a small question.
It is a question that changes everything.

saw.intro: There is a silence that settles in when a diagnosis arrives and no one truly knows what to do with it.
saw.point1: Parents bewildered by reports that do not speak to one another.
saw.point2: Educators overwhelmed by strategies that rarely survive the real pace of the classroom.
saw.point3: Capable professionals, yet fragmented — each one looking at a part of the child, almost never at the whole.
saw.point4: And in the middle of it all, children simply trying to exist.
saw.quote:
The widest gap was never between the child and the world.
It was between lived experience and the language used to explain it.

believe.item1Line1: Before any curriculum, before any intervention,
believe.item1Highlight: the world needs to be habitable.
believe.item1Line2: Safe. Predictable. With a rhythm of its own.
believe.item2Line1: Before the word, there is the gesture.
believe.item2Line2: Before the concept, there is experience.
believe.item2Line3: Before correcting, we need to understand.
believe.item3Title: The world comes before the curriculum.
believe.item3Line1: No content makes sense while the world is still lived as fragmented.

doing.body1: GrowKind exists to do that translation.
doing.body2: A living ground where development can be accompanied with rigour, time and attention to what is real.
doing.body3: Here, observation finds structure. Professionals find a shared language. And those who walk alongside the child find support for the realities of everyday life.
doing.quote:
Development does not happen only inside the child.
It emerges in the field that forms between child, adult and environment.
doing.closing: When that field finds structure, development stops being a problem to solve and becomes a process that can be accompanied.

forWhom.parentsBody: Who want to understand before they act.
forWhom.educatorsBody: Who teach without erasing who the child is.
forWhom.prosBody: Who know that development begins before technique.
forWhom.allBody: Who believe that accompanying is more demanding — and more powerful — than correcting.

home.adultLine:
When the adult learns to see,
the child is no longer alone
in the process.

bookSection.title:
Where the World
Is Born Between Us
bookSection.coverAlt: Where the World Is Born Between Us — João Pereira
bookSection.body1: It is not a manual. It is a shift in perspective.
bookSection.body2: Rather than explaining autism as a diagnosis, this book follows development as a living process — something that happens in the body, in time and in relationship.
bookSection.body3: Every gesture, repetition or pause is read as an attempt to keep balance. Nothing here is treated as an error to eliminate, but as a movement that asks to be understood.

rdfSection.title: Relational Development Framework
rdfSection.subtitle:
A way of reading what is happening
before any decision about how to respond.
rdfSection.body: The RDF is not a therapy. It is not a protocol. It is a lens — a way of organising how the adult observes, reads and positions themselves within the child's relational field.
rdfSection.closing: Development is not solved. Development is accompanied.

meta.title: Where the World Is Born Between Us — The Book
meta.description: It is not a manual. It is a shift in perspective. Rather than explaining autism as a diagnosis, this book follows development as a living process. By João Pereira.
meta.ogAlt: Where the World Is Born Between Us — João Pereira
keywords: Where the World Is Born Between Us

hero.subtitle: A shared path in childhood autism
hero.tagline: It is not a manual. It is a shift in perspective.
hero.description: Not every behaviour asks for correction. Some ask for reading, time and presence. This book follows development as a living process — something that happens in the body, in time and in relationship.
hero.coverAlt: Cover of the book Where the World Is Born Between Us — João Pereira

about.body1: It is not a book about autism as a diagnosis. It is a book about what happens before intervention — about what asks to be read before being corrected.
about.body2: Every gesture, repetition or pause is read as an attempt to keep balance. Nothing here is treated as an error to eliminate, but as a movement that asks to be understood.
about.body3: Throughout the reading, the adult stops chasing quick answers and starts coordinating processes. Not to control. But to sustain.
about.body4: If, by the end, you are left without immediate solutions — but with a sharper eye and a steadier presence beside the child — then this book has done its job.

toc.subtitle: 20 chapters organised in three parts — from feeling to doing, from doing to relationship.

families.title: For parents and families
families.description: Who want to understand before they act — and accompany before they correct.

educators.title: For educators
educators.description: Who teach without erasing who the child is — and who look for a solid professional lens.

professionals.title: For professionals
professionals.description: Who know that development begins before technique — and who want to coordinate rather than fragment.

excerpt: There is a question that runs through this entire book — not as a topic to be solved, but as an orientation for the way we look.
What is happening when the child acts the way they act?
Not "how do I make this stop?" — but "what is this telling us?"
It is a simple distinction. But it changes everything.
When the question is "how do I make this stop?", the behaviour becomes a problem. The adult becomes a fixer. And the child becomes someone who needs to be corrected.

When the question is "what is this telling us?", the behaviour becomes a message. The adult becomes a reader. And the child becomes someone who is trying, with the resources they have, to keep something organised inside.
This book is built from that second question.

It is not a manual. It offers no protocols or lists of strategies. It does not promise to turn the child into someone easier to accompany. What it proposes is more demanding — and, in my experience, more lasting: a shift in perspective.

Over nearly three decades accompanying autistic children, families and educators — in Brazil, in Portugal, in the United Kingdom — I have learned that the greatest obstacle is rarely in the child. It lies in the distance between what the child lives and the language the adults around them use to explain what they see.

That distance produces misunderstandings. Interventions that arrive at the wrong moment. Demands that arrive before the field is ready to receive them. Exhausted adults trying to solve what needed to be accompanied.
This book is an attempt to reduce that distance.

Each chapter follows a moment of development — not as a stage to complete, but as a territory to recognise. There are things the child's body communicates before any word. There are things the environment offers or withdraws before any instruction. There are things the adult's presence conveys before any technique.
It is in that "before" that this book settles.
data.quote: Development is not solved. It is accompanied.

meta.title: Relational Development Framework — RDF
meta.description: A way of reading what is happening between the child and the environment, before any decision about how to respond. Not a therapy. Not a protocol. A lens.

diagramaMeta.title: RDF Diagram — The Relational Field
diagramaMeta.description: Explore the interactive diagram of the Relational Development Framework — the three movements and the relational field between child, adult and environment.

hero.entryQuote: What is seen from the outside is usually called behaviour. What is not seen is the immense effort to keep some continuity inside.
hero.questionShift: The question stops being “how do I make this stop?” and begins to become “what is this telling us?”
hero.description: A way of reading what is happening between the child and the environment — before any decision about how to respond. It is not a therapy. It is not a protocol. It is a lens.

question.body: This question is the starting point of every RDF reading. It does not begin with what to do. It begins with what is happening. It is a simple inversion — but it changes everything that comes after.

diagram.subtitle: Click each element to see what the adult reads — and each movement to understand when and how.
diagram.arcRight: M1 · Inhabiting the World · Being Ground
diagram.arcBottomA: M3 · Meeting the Other · Being Mediator
diagram.arcLeft: M2 · Acting in the World · Being Structure

extras.child: What is happening with this child's nervous system right now?
extras.adult: But the adult's presence is always the third element of the field. What am I adding to the field right now?

movements.warning: <critical>Critical:</critical> The movements are not stages of progress or diagnostic categories. They are reading lenses. The same child may need different movements over the course of the same day. The adult does not “move on” from one movement to the next — they read what the field is asking for right now.
movements.transversalBadge: Belongs to the adult's field — not to the child's development
movements.transversalDesc: Ensures coherence among all the adults involved — educators, family, therapists. It is not leadership. It is coherence of reading over time. The axis-adult has observational authority: they see the child's real day, which no clinical report captures.
movements.transversalQuote: The adult does not become a specialist. They become an axis. They are the one who notices the micro-changes that never appear in reports.

whatIsNot.title: What the RDF is — and what it is not
whatIsNot.intro: This distinction matters. The RDF is often mistaken for a therapy or a method. It is something else: it operates one level before — in the reading that precedes any decision about how to respond.

axiom.eyebrow: For those who want to go deeper
axiom.title: The scientific foundations
axiom.intro: The RDF is not an intuition — it rests on a solid scientific base across five pillars of research. For those who want to know more.
axiom.pillarsIntro: The RDF is not a structured intuition. Every field movement and every operating principle is grounded in published research. The five pillars below are the scientific foundations of the framework.
axiom.approachesIntro: The RDF does not compete with existing approaches. It operates one level before — the reading of the field that precedes any decision about how to respond. This distinction removes the need to choose between frameworks.
axiom.canonical: The canonical formulation: the RDF is the level before the decision about how to respond. It does not replace what the professional already knows. It organises the reading that precedes what the professional will decide to do.
axiom.glossaryIntro: The RDF has a language of its own — precise and shareable. This glossary is the entry point. In the courses, the language is deepened in professional and family contexts.

cta.body: The lens is available on this page. What the courses deepen is its application — with tools, real cases and guided reflection. For professionals and for families.

data.question: What is happening between this child and the environment — and where does the adult fit right now?
data.axiom: Development is not solved. Development is accompanied.

child.title: The Child
child.subtitle: start
child.role: Behaviour is always an effect — never a starting point
child.desc: The RDF begins with the child — but not with the behaviour they show. It begins with the internal state that produces that behaviour.
reads: Regulatory state — regulated, mobilised, overloaded, hypoactivated
Sensory profile — what arrives strong, what arrives soft
Available capacity — what the field can process right now
Energy — it is not laziness, it is the real capacity available
child.quote: "What is seen from the outside is usually called behaviour. What is not seen is the immense effort to keep some continuity inside."

environment.title: The Environment
environment.subtitle: participates
environment.role: The space modulates the field before any instruction
environment.desc: The environment is not the backdrop — it is an active participant that supports or overloads the nervous system before any word is spoken.
reads: Sensory stimulation — light, sound, density, temperature
Spatial organisation — edges, predictability, points of support
Predictability of the routine — what the body already knows comes next
Demands of the moment — what is being asked right now
environment.quote: "When entering a space, let your gaze travel slowly across the environment. Sometimes adjusting a single detail is enough to restore comfort."

adult.title: The Adult
adult.subtitle: reads and adjusts
adult.role: Presence is the intervention — before any technique
adult.desc: The adult reads themselves last — because they are the easiest to ignore.
reads: Bodily presence and rhythm — what the body communicates before words
Tone of voice and speed — the child's nervous system reads this directly
Transmitted expectations — what is implicit in my presence
My own emotional state — my regulation is my intervention
adult.quote: "The adult's own regulation is the intervention. A mature nervous system that lends organisation."

m1.title: Inhabiting the World
m1.when: When the field asks for regulation
m1.desc: The adult becomes a regulating presence before any instruction. Regulation BEFORE instruction — always. Never insist on a task while the field is dysregulated.
items: Reduce stimulation before any verbal instruction
Adjust the rhythm and proximity of your own body
Remain when everything speeds up — presence is the intervention
m1.quote: "Being ground is everything the other needs in order to exist in the space again."

m2.title: Acting in the World
m2.when: When the field asks for structure
m2.desc: Offers the framing that makes action possible. Lends the beginning without doing it for the child. Three moments: starting, sustaining, finishing.
items: Start — lend the first step without walking the whole path
Sustain — become an anchoring presence when the action dissolves
Finish — signal transitions before they arrive
Structure that frees ≠ rigidity that traps
m2.quote: "Lending the beginning is not doing it for the child. It is stepping in exactly where the weight is greatest."

m3.title: Meeting the Other
m3.when: When the field asks for relationship
m3.desc: Mediates the encounter without forcing or substituting. Three levels: the other as obstacle → reference → a mind of their own.
items: Level 1: the other as functional obstacle — do not force interaction
Level 2: the other as responsive reference — give observation time
Level 3: the other as a mind of their own — mediate the encounter
Never apply Level 3 mediation to a child at Level 1
m3.quote: "The other does not begin to exist when they are understood. They begin to exist when they interfere."

m4.title: Coordinating the Field
m4.when: Transversal movement — belongs to the adult
m4.desc: Ensures coherence among all the adults. It is not leadership — it is coherence of reading.
items: Axis between educators, family and therapists
Holds observational authority that no clinical report has
Sees the child's real day — the in-between that others do not see
Not the most qualified — the one who holds the data the others do not have
m4.quote: "The adult does not become a specialist. They become an axis."

isNot: a substitute for existing approaches (PBS, ABA, DIR...)
a sequence of stages to go through
is: a lens for reading the relational field
the level before the decision about how to respond
a methodology that can be deepened through training
what precedes any intervention — not what replaces it
a way of reading that changes what is offered

pillars.label: Pillar 1
pillars.title: Neurodiversity and Diagnosis
pillars.desc: The RDF starts from the premise that a diagnosis is information — not destiny. Happé's and Baron-Cohen's research on central coherence and autistic cognition shows that processing differences are not deficits to correct, but distinct profiles of neurological organisation. The DSM-5-TR is used as a nosological reference, not as a response map. The RDF operates after the diagnosis — and before the intervention.

pillars.label: Pillar 2
pillars.title: Sensory Processing
pillars.desc: The environment is an active participant in the relational field — not a backdrop. Each child's sensory thresholds (hyperresponsiveness, hyporesponsiveness, sensory seeking, avoidance) determine what the nervous system processes before any verbal instruction arrives. Winnie Dunn's work on sensory profiles grounds the RDF's environmental reading: adjusting the space is intervening.

pillars.label: Pillar 3
pillars.title: Autonomic Nervous System Regulation
pillars.refs: Stephen W. Porges — The Polyvagal Theory (2011)
pillars.desc: Porges' Polyvagal Theory describes how the autonomic nervous system organises physiological states on three levels: safety (ventral vagal system), mobilisation (sympathetic system) and immobilisation (dorsal vagal). Learning, attention and social relating are only possible from a state of neurological safety. This is the scientific basis of RDF Movement 1 — regulation before instruction, always.

pillars.label: Pillar 4
pillars.title: Attachment and Relational Development
pillars.refs: Bowlby (1969, 1973) · Ainsworth et al. (1978) · Greenspan & Wieder (2006)
pillars.desc: Bowlby's and Ainsworth's attachment theory establishes that the adult functions as a secure base — a condition of relational development, not merely emotional support. The regulated adult regulates the child's nervous system through presence. Greenspan and Wieder (DIR/Floortime) deepen how levels of relational development build sequentially — the scientific basis of RDF Movement 3 and of the three levels of mediation.

pillars.label: Pillar 5
pillars.title: Sociocognitive Development and Co-regulation
pillars.desc: Vygotsky demonstrated that development happens in the zone of proximal development — between what the child can do alone and what they can do with adult presence. Tomasello deepened the role of joint attention as the foundation of social cognition. Siegel and Schore showed that co-regulation is a neurobiological process: the adult's nervous system organises the child's nervous system. The adult is not an external mediator — they are a condition of development.

approaches.name: PBS / PBIS
approaches.relation: The RDF operates before: it reads the field that produces the behaviour before any support plan.

approaches.name: ABA
approaches.relation: The RDF neither replaces nor competes: it precedes with the reading. A well-read field informs better decisions in any methodology.

approaches.name: DIR / Floortime
approaches.level: Affective interaction — following the child, building relationship
approaches.relation: Shares the RDF's relational premise. Movement 3 (Being Mediator) is directly grounded in the DIR progression. Complementary.

approaches.name: Polyvagal Theory
approaches.relation: Direct scientific basis of Movement 1. The RDF operationalises Polyvagal Theory in educational and family contexts.

approaches.name: Sensory Processing
approaches.relation: Basis of environmental reading in the RDF. The sensory profile informs what the environment is offering the nervous system.

approaches.name: Emotional Regulation
approaches.relation: The RDF operates one level before: regulating the field (adult + environment) creates the conditions for emotional regulation to be possible.

glossary.def: The space that emerges when child, environment and adult are in simultaneous relation. It is not a physical place — it is where development happens. The field is read, not diagnosed.
Observing and interpreting what is happening among the three elements — child, environment, adult — before any decision about how to respond. It is the central professional act of the RDF.

Types of need that emerge in the field: regulation, structure, relationship. They are reading lenses — not sequential stages, not diagnostic categories. The same child may need different movements over the same day.
The adult's role in Movement 1. Becoming a regulating presence before any verbal instruction. Regulation of the field before any task.
The adult's role in Movement 2. Offering framing that makes action possible — not control, but containment that frees. Lending the beginning without doing it for the child.
The adult's role in Movement 3. Mediating the encounter between the child and the other without forcing or substituting. Entering the field without taking the child's space.
The adult's role in the Transversal Movement. Ensuring coherence of reading among all the adults involved — educators, family, therapists. It is not leadership. It is coherence over time.
The adult's nervous system organises the child's nervous system through presence. It is not a technique — it is a neurobiological condition. The adult's regulation is the intervention.
A structured record of observations made by parents and support professionals over time. Shareable with therapists and teams. The TA sees the child's real day, which no clinical report captures.

diagramPage.heroSubtitle: Click each movement to explore what the field is asking for — and what the adult can offer.

paragraphs: When the field asks for regulation, the adult becomes ground. The child's nervous system needs to find stability before anything else.
The adult does not instruct — they regulate. Presence is the intervention. No technique replaces the regulated adult who simply stays.
Foundation: Polyvagal Theory (Porges) — the autonomic nervous system needs a state of neurophysiological safety before any learning is possible.
When the field asks for structure, the adult becomes structure. The child needs predictability and organisation in order to act.
The adult offers the framing that makes action possible — not as control, but as containment that frees. Routine is not rigidity — it is predictable ground.
The difference between correcting the behaviour and organising the field that produced it.
When the field asks for relationship, the adult becomes the mediator of the encounter. This movement has an internal progression that is pedagogically critical.
The other as functional obstacle → the other as responsive reference → the other as a mind of their own. Many adults are at the first point. Starting with Theory of Mind loses them.
The RDF starts where the field is — not where we would like it to be.
This movement belongs to the adult's field — not to the child's development. It is transversal to all the movements, neither sequential nor final.
The adult who coordinates the field keeps coherence between what they observe, what they decide and what they communicate — to the family, the school, the therapists.
In practice, it is often the mother, the father or the TA who fulfils this role — ensuring that the reading of the field is shared and coherent among all the adults accompanying the child.
